anethole.title: Anethole
anethole.summary: Anethole is the defining sweet-spice aromatic in anise and fennel, and the key driver of classical absinthe's louche behavior.
anethole.sensory: Sweet licorice, anisic warmth, polished fennel sweetness.

biochemistry.text: Review literature describes anethole as an orally handled phenylpropene that undergoes oxidative metabolism and conjugation after absorption.
Mechanistic work also identifies trans-anethole as a TRPA1 agonist, while broader pharmacology reviews discuss anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity largely in preclinical settings.

anetholeReview.title: Dietary anethole: a systematic review of its protective effects against metabolic syndrome and related disorders

anetholeTrpa1.title: trans-Anethole of fennel oil is a selective and nonelectrophilic agonist of TRPA1

fenchone.title: Fenchone
fenchone.summary: Fenchone provides the dry, camphoraceous counterweight that keeps fennel and anise profiles from collapsing into candy.
fenchone.sensory: Dry herbal camphor, bitter fennel, cool mineral sharpness.

biochemistry.text: Fenchone is treated in the literature as a small lipophilic monoterpene, with preclinical work examining diuretic and safety profiles after oral exposure.
Its biological literature is still limited and leans toward preclinical antispasmodic and antimicrobial models rather than human functional evidence.

fenchoneDiuretic.title: Fenchone, a monoterpene: toxicity and diuretic profiling in rats

fenchoneSpasmolytic.title: In silico and ex vivo studies on the spasmolytic activities of fenchone

thujone.title: Thujone
thujone.summary: Thujone is one of the best-known wormwood-associated terpenes and a major symbolic marker in absinthe discourse.
thujone.sensory: Dry green bitterness, resin, sage-like edge, austere lift.

biochemistry.text: The best-established biochemical claim for thujone is negative modulation of GABAA receptor function, which explains its long toxicology history in absinthe discussions.
Detoxification studies also show species- and isoform-dependent cytochrome P450 oxidation, so metabolism matters as much as receptor pharmacology.

thujoneGaba.title: Alpha-thujone (the active component of absinthe): GABAA receptor modulation and metabolic detoxification

thujoneDetox.title: Detoxification of alpha- and beta-thujones (the active ingredients of absinthe): site specificity and species differences in cytochrome P450 oxidation in vitro and in vivo

linalool.title: Linalool
linalool.summary: Linalool broadens harsh herbal structures into something more perfumed and breathable.
linalool.sensory: Floral citrus, lavender-like softness, polished lift.

biochemistry.text: Linalool is metabolized to oxygenated and acetylated products, and some of those metabolites retain GABAA-relevant activity in vitro.
Broader reviews discuss anxiolytic, anti-inflammatory, and mood-related literature, but most of that evidence remains preclinical or route-dependent.

linaloolMetabolism.title: Metabolic products of linalool and modulation of GABAA receptors

linaloolReview.title: Linalool as a therapeutic and medicinal tool in depression treatment

citral.title: Citral
citral.summary: Citral is the sharp lemon leaf cue behind melissa and lemon verbena styles.
citral.sensory: Lemon zest, green rind, bright volatile citrus.

biochemistry.text: Citral is mainly discussed as a reactive monoterpene aldehyde whose biological activity is dominated by antimicrobial and membrane-active chemistry.
The evidence base around citral is largely preclinical, which is why this site treats it as a freshness-associated aroma cue rather than a beverage function claim.

citralReview.title: Mechanisms and applications of citral's antimicrobial properties in food preservation

chlorophylls.title: Chlorophylls
chlorophylls.summary: Chlorophylls matter less as aroma molecules than as the visual anchor for naturally colored verte styles.
chlorophylls.sensory: Visual rather than aromatic: deep herbal green, opaque botanical signal.

biochemistry.text: Reviews of chlorophyll digestion and absorption describe substantial transformation during digestion, with limited emphasis on intact pigment persistence in circulation.
Most human-interest literature is therefore about pigment stability, digestion, and antioxidant context rather than direct receptor pharmacology.

chlorophyllReview.title: Enhancing health benefits through chlorophylls and chlorophyll-rich agro-food: a comprehensive review

chlorophyllFoodReview.title: Chlorophylls as natural bioactive compounds existing in food by-products: a critical review

alpha-pinene.title: Alpha-pinene
alpha-pinene.summary: Alpha-pinene creates crisp lift and makes bitter herbal formulas feel high and airy instead of muddy.
alpha-pinene.sensory: Pine needle, resin, dry forest top note.

biochemistry.text: Alpha-pinene is absorbed and metabolized into oxygenated terpene derivatives, with human and animal exposure literature focusing on general volatile-terpene pharmacokinetics.
The pharmacology literature often explores anti-inflammatory, bronchodilatory, and neuroactive associations, but the evidence remains mixed and mostly preclinical.

natVolatiles.title: Pharmacology of natural volatiles and essential oils in food, therapy, and disease prophylaxis

pineneReview.title: Therapeutic potential of α- and β-pinene: a miracle gift of nature

limonene.title: Limonene
limonene.summary: Limonene adds a broad citrus glow that can sharpen structure without tasting like juice.
limonene.sensory: Orange peel, citrus oil, bright terpene bloom.

biochemistry.text: Limonene undergoes oxidative metabolism to multiple downstream metabolites, which is why biomarker and metabolite papers matter more than unchanged-compound measurements alone.
Reviews also discuss anti-inflammatory, gastrointestinal, and chemopreventive associations, but again these are mostly preclinical rather than beverage-relevant human outcomes.

limoneneMetabolites.title: Identification of D-limonene metabolites by LC-HRMS

natVolatiles.title: Pharmacology of natural volatiles and essential oils in food, therapy, and disease prophylaxis

terpinen-4-ol.title: Terpinen-4-ol
terpinen-4-ol.summary: Terpinen-4-ol is a quieter structural terpene that helps hold herbal and citrus notes together.
terpinen-4-ol.sensory: Soft herb, tea-tree-like cleanliness, muted camphor warmth.

biochemistry.text: Terpinen-4-ol is best documented as a major tea tree constituent with antimicrobial activity and broader essential-oil pharmacology relevance.
Anti-inflammatory reviews also describe terpinen-4-ol activity in cell and animal models, but that evidence is not a basis for alcohol-function messaging.

terpinen4olTeaTree.title: Melaleuca alternifolia (tea tree) oil: a review of antimicrobial and other medicinal properties

terpinen4olAntiInflamm.title: A review on anti-inflammatory activity of monoterpenes

rosmarinic-acid.title: Rosmarinic acid
rosmarinic-acid.summary: Rosmarinic acid is one of the major phenolics behind the cooling, herbal depth of lemon balm and related Lamiaceae herbs.
rosmarinic-acid.sensory: Not strongly aromatic on its own; contributes green, tannic-herbal depth.

biochemistry.text: Rosmarinic acid reviews consistently note limited oral bioavailability together with measurable intestinal permeability and downstream phenolic metabolism.
The larger pharmacology literature emphasizes antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, largely outside the context of beverage alcohol.

rosmarinicReview.title: A comprehensive review of rosmarinic acid: from phytochemistry to pharmacology and its new insight

rosmarinicBioavailability.title: Evaluation of the intestinal permeability of rosmarinic acid from Thunbergia laurifolia leaf water extract in a Caco-2 cell model

anthocyanins.title: Anthocyanins
anthocyanins.summary: Anthocyanins give rouge systems their jewel-like red-violet tone and respond visibly to acidity.
anthocyanins.sensory: Visual first: ruby, garnet, magenta cues; lightly tannic-fruit context.

biochemistry.text: Anthocyanins have low oral bioavailability and are extensively transformed during digestion, absorption, and circulation.
As a result, much of the human-biochemistry discussion concerns metabolites and signaling associations rather than long persistence of intact pigments.

anthocyaninBioavailability.title: A review of factors affecting anthocyanin bioavailability

anthocyaninMetabolism.title: Research progress on absorption, metabolism, and biological activities of anthocyanins

geraniol.title: Geraniol
geraniol.summary: Geraniol is one of the clearest floral cues in rose-forward systems and helps rouge stay perfumed instead of merely tart.
geraniol.sensory: Rose petal, lychee, sweet floral citrus.

biochemistry.text: Geraniol pharmacokinetic reviews report oral metabolism to conjugated and free metabolites, with much of the direct data coming from preclinical models.
The broader bioactivity literature centers on anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial potential, but the relevant evidence is still mostly preclinical.

geraniolPk.title: Geraniol pharmacokinetics, bioavailability and its multiple effects on the liver

ternatins.title: Ternatins
ternatins.summary: Ternatins are the signature pigments of butterfly pea flower and the engine behind Detour's chromatic theater.
ternatins.sensory: Primarily visual: saturated blue with pH-sensitive movement toward violet and purple.

biochemistry.text: Ternatins are acylated anthocyanins from butterfly pea, and the literature emphasizes their pH-dependent blue-to-violet color behavior and stability chemistry.
Direct human-biochemistry data for ternatins are sparse; the safest inference is that they likely follow anthocyanin-style digestion and metabolite formation rather than remaining intact for long.

butterflyAnthocyanin.title: Anthocyanins from Clitoria ternatea flower: biosynthesis, extraction, stability, antioxidant activity, and applications

anthocyaninMetabolism.title: Research progress on absorption, metabolism, and biological activities of anthocyanins

verbascoside.title: Verbascoside
verbascoside.summary: Verbascoside is a useful marker for the denser phenolic side of lemon verbena-style herbs.
verbascoside.sensory: Tea-like phenolic depth, herbal bitterness, dried-leaf structure.

biochemistry.text: Verbascoside reviews describe antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective literature together with pharmacokinetic work on the broader acteoside/verbascoside family.
Mechanistically it is better treated as a phenylpropanoid glycoside with broad extract-level bioactivity literature than as a validated human receptor ligand.

verbascosidePk.title: The pharmacokinetic property and pharmacological activity of acteoside: a review

pinocamphone.title: Pinocamphone
pinocamphone.summary: Pinocamphone contributes some of hyssop's sharper medicinal and resinous edge.
pinocamphone.sensory: Camphor, resin, medicinal herb, austere lift.

biochemistry.text: Hyssop reviews identify pinocamphone and isopinocamphone as major ketones and repeatedly flag dose-dependent neurotoxicity and seizure-related safety concerns.
That makes pinocamphone primarily a safety-managed aroma constituent rather than a compound for positive health framing.

hyssopReview.title: Hyssopus essential oil: an update of its phytochemistry, biological activities, and safety profile

hyssopGenusReview.title: The genus Hyssopus: traditional use, phytochemicals and pharmacological activities

citric-acid.title: Citric acid
citric-acid.summary: Citric acid is a direct route to brightness and helps red and blue botanical systems feel alive.
citric-acid.sensory: Sour snap, lifted fruit perception, cleaner finish.

biochemistry.text: Citrate is a central tricarboxylic-acid-cycle intermediate, so citric acid sits close to core mammalian energy metabolism even though dietary citric acid is not a specialized signaling supplement.
In drinks, its physiological relevance is dominated by acidity and salivary response rather than bespoke pharmacology.

tcaReview.title: Regulation and function of the mammalian tricarboxylic acid cycle

gentiopicroside.title: Gentiopicroside
gentiopicroside.summary: Gentiopicroside represents the kind of structured bitterness that makes gentian useful in aperitif architecture.
gentiopicroside.sensory: Firm bitter line, rooty dryness, palate-tightening persistence.

biochemistry.text: Gentiopicroside is a major bitter secoiridoid of gentian, and reviews place it in the overlap between bitter-taste chemistry and broader preclinical anti-inflammatory or gastrointestinal literature.
For this project, its most defensible biochemical role is bitterness perception and aperitif structure rather than therapeutic positioning.

gentiopicrosideReview.title: Gentiopicroside: an insight into its pharmacological significance and mechanisms of action

gentianaceaeReview.title: Gentianaceae family-derived bioactive compounds: therapeutic prospects and mechanisms

menthol.title: Menthol
menthol.summary: Menthol creates a trigeminal cooling sensation that can make a spirit feel calmer, cleaner, and more spacious.
menthol.sensory: Cooling mint, clear breath, icy herbal lift.

biochemistry.text: Menthol is one of the canonical agonists of TRPM8, which is why it produces cooling without any actual drop in temperature.
Clinical and topical-product reviews treat menthol primarily as a sensory and symptomatic cooling agent rather than a systemic wellness compound.

mentholTRPM8.title: Identification of a cold receptor reveals a general role for TRP channels in thermosensation

mentholReview.title: Looking back to move forward: the current state of research on topical analgesics containing menthol or camphor

cineole.title: 1,8-Cineole
cineole.summary: 1,8-Cineole brings a clean, eucalyptus-like lift that helps alpine herbs read cool and spacious.
cineole.sensory: Cool camphor, eucalyptus, lifted herbal air.

biochemistry.text: 1,8-Cineole has a substantial respiratory and anti-inflammatory literature, but those claims belong to medicine or preclinical pharmacology rather than beverage messaging.
What matters most here is that the same chemistry that drives that literature also makes cineole read as cool, airy, and alpine in aroma.

cineoleReview.title: Modes of action of 1,8-cineol in infections and inflammation

borneol.title: Borneol
borneol.summary: Borneol gives alpine herbs a softer camphoraceous height without the hard edge of stronger ketones.
borneol.sensory: Camphor-soft, resinous, dry alpine top note.

biochemistry.text: Borneol reviews focus on permeability enhancement, central-nervous-system delivery research, and adjunct anti-inflammatory or analgesic applications.
That literature is why borneol is chemically interesting, but in this project it is still treated as an aroma and texture cue, not a functional beverage claim.

borneolReview.title: Role of borneol as enhancer in drug formulation: a review

borneolCns.title: Borneol, a novel agent that improves central nervous system drug delivery by enhancing blood-brain barrier permeability

hotrienol.title: Hotrienol
hotrienol.summary: Hotrienol is a delicate floral top-note compound associated with airy blossom character.
hotrienol.sensory: Honeyed floral vapor, blossom-like sweetness, soft halo.

biochemistry.text: The literature on hotrienol is overwhelmingly aroma-analytical: it is discussed as a floral volatile marker rather than a well-characterized human bioactive.
Accordingly, this site treats hotrienol as an olfactory softness cue and explicitly avoids stronger physiological claims that the literature does not support well.

hotrienolMarker.title: Hotrienol as a marker of the Zweigelt variety

hotrienolAroma.title: Analysis of aroma-active volatiles in an SDE extract of white tea

terpinyl-acetate.title: Terpinyl acetate
terpinyl-acetate.summary: Terpinyl acetate is one of the compounds that makes cardamom feel sparkling, lifted, and expensive.
terpinyl-acetate.sensory: Sweet spice, cool lift, perfumed cardamom brightness.

biochemistry.text: Cardamom reviews consistently identify alpha-terpinyl acetate as a major constituent and discuss early antimicrobial and essential-oil bioactivity literature around it.
The direct human-biochemistry evidence is thin, so it is safest to present terpinyl acetate here as a high-impact aroma ester rather than a validated functional agent.

cardamomReview.title: Recent advances in the extraction, chemical composition, and biological activities of cardamom

cardamomEO.title: Effects of essential oils of Elettaria cardamomum grown in India and Guatemala on the growth of Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa

paradols.title: Paradols
paradols.summary: Paradols represent the warming pungency of grains of paradise and related spice materials.
paradols.sensory: Warm peppery heat, salivation, spicy glow.

biochemistry.text: Paradols belong to the pungent ginger chemistry family, where reviews describe anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective research that is still mostly preclinical.
Their most immediate human effect in a drink is trigeminal warmth and salivation, which is sensory territory rather than a health claim.

gingerReview.title: A critical review of ginger's antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and bioactive constituents

paradolNeuro.title: Neuroprotective effects of 6-shogaol and its metabolite, 6-paradol, in a Parkinson disease mouse model

sabinene.title: Sabinene
sabinene.summary: Sabinene gives juniper its dry, biting, gin-adjacent lift.
sabinene.sensory: Peppery pine, dry resin, bright conifer bite.

biochemistry.text: Sabinene appears constantly in phytochemical and terpene reviews, but direct human-biochemistry evidence is much thinner than for limonene, pinene, or menthol.
That is why this site treats sabinene mainly as a structural aroma hydrocarbon rather than attaching broad functional claims to it.

sabinaReview.title: Phytochemistry, pharmacology, and traditional medicine of Juniperus sabina

terpenesReview.title: Terpenes and terpenoids as main bioactive compounds of essential oils

myrcene.title: Myrcene
myrcene.summary: Myrcene deepens resinous and citrus systems, giving them body and a slightly humid vegetal floor.
myrcene.sensory: Resin, damp herb, terpene depth, rounded volatile body.

biochemistry.text: Myrcene is usually discussed in broader terpene reviews that summarize preclinical analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and sedative associations along with standard lipophilic terpene handling.
For beverage design, the defensible point is not those prospective bioactivities but myrcene's effect on aromatic depth and persistence.

terpenesReview.title: Terpenes and terpenoids as main bioactive compounds of essential oils

myrceneReview.title: Terpenes/terpenoids in cannabis: are they important?

natVolatiles.title: Pharmacology of natural volatiles and essential oils in food, therapy, and disease prophylaxis

camphor.title: Camphor
camphor.summary: Camphor brings austere medicinal brightness and a clean alpine edge.
camphor.sensory: Sharp medicinal coolness, resin, alpine austerity.

biochemistry.text: Camphor has a long medical and toxicological history, and modern reviews still frame it as a potent sensory irritant with meaningful safety considerations at higher exposure.
That is why camphor works best here as a tightly controlled precision note rather than anything that should be promoted for health value.

camphorReview.title: Camphor: a fumigant during the Black Death and a coveted fragrant wood in ancient Egypt and Babylon

camphorHazards.title: Camphor's therapeutic uses and potential hazards: an in-depth review

carotenoids.title: Carotenoids
carotenoids.summary: Carotenoids are warm yellow-orange plant pigments that can support an amber botanical visual cue.
carotenoids.sensory: Visual first: gold, orange, amber, dried-petal warmth rather than a dominant aroma note.

biochemistry.text: Dietary carotenoids are discussed mainly as lipophilic pigments and antioxidant-associated compounds, with human relevance depending on compound identity, matrix, and absorption.
For this absinthe context, the defensible role is color design rather than a physiological promise.

carotenoidReview.title: Carotenoids: considerations for human nutrition and health

lutein.title: Lutein
lutein.summary: Lutein is a yellow xanthophyll carotenoid associated with marigold and calendula-style petal color.
lutein.sensory: Visual contribution: golden-yellow warmth and dried-petal color association.

biochemistry.text: Lutein is widely studied as a dietary xanthophyll, especially in nutrition literature around ocular pigment status, but that does not translate into beverage-effect positioning.
Here it is used as a color and botanical-context marker for calendula, not as a health claim.

luteinReview.title: Lutein and zeaxanthin and their roles in age-related macular degeneration
